metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete propagation channel for the validated proxy identity from the ASGI middleware to the TokenVerifier-shaped component (e.g., ASGI `scope` dict key, a `contextvars.ContextVar`, or a sanitized internal header), and confirm that choice is compatible with how S2's composite invokes verifiers under FastMCP's async dispatch.
findings.finding: The mechanism by which the ASGI middleware propagates the validated proxy identity to the downstream TokenVerifier-shaped component is unspecified and internally inconsistent: the plan calls it a 'request-scoped principal' (implying a side-channel like an ASGI scope dict key or a contextvar) but also uses the phrase 'strip-then-repopulate' (implying the middleware re-injects a sanitized internal header). These are structurally different choices with different failure modes: an internal header may be stripped by FastMCP before the verifier sees it; a contextvar may not propagate across async task boundaries that S2's composite uses; an ASGI scope dict mutation requires both the middleware and the verifier to operate on the same scope object, which holds only if FastMCP passes the raw scope through. Without specifying the propagation channel, an implementer may choose an approach that silently breaks identity flow without any test catching it (because the unit tests exercise the middleware and verifier directly, not necessarily through FastMCP's actual request-dispatch chain).
findings.location: Approach & rationale / What section — 'request-scoped principal' + 'strip-then-repopulate' + 'The verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'
findings.suggested_fix: Choose an ASGI `scope` dict key (e.g., `scope['rebar.proxy_principal']`) written by the middleware and read by the verifier — this is the canonical ASGI pattern for intra-request state, requires no import of async-context machinery, and is visible to any downstream ASGI component operating on the same request scope object. State this explicitly in the Approach section and in the AC for the unit-test that exercises the integrated middleware→composite→verifier chain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact ASGI state key (e.g., `scope['state']['_proxy_verified_identity']`) and its type that the middleware writes and the verifier reads, co-locating this contract in the plan's Approach section alongside both component descriptions.
findings.finding: The middleware writes a 'request-scoped principal' that the verifier reads, but the inter-component communication protocol (the ASGI scope/state key name, its type, and its lifecycle) is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale: 'The middleware runs before the MCP app…extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; the verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-section 'Inter-component contract' specifying: the ASGI state key (e.g., `request.state.proxy_identity: str | None`), who writes it (middleware, after constant-time compare succeeds), who reads it (verifier, in its verify() method), and that it is always initialized to None by the middleware (even on failure) to prevent cross-request bleed.

findings.finding: The plan asserts the proxy-guard ASGI middleware 'runs before the MCP app' to strip/repopulate headers before the S2 composite auth handler sees the request, but does not name the FastMCP API used to insert it. The specific hook point — whether to wrap FastMCP's underlying Starlette app, use a FastMCP-level middleware API, or wrap the ASGI callable returned by `mcp.streamable_http_app()` — is unspecified. If the middleware is inserted AFTER FastMCP's own OAuth bearer-auth handler (which is where the S2 composite runs), the strip-then-repopulate guarantee is lost: a spoofed `X-Forwarded-User` header would be evaluated by the composite before the guard can strip it.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, specify: 'The proxy-guard middleware wraps the top-level ASGI app (`server.asgi_app` or the equivalent FastMCP streamable-http callable) so it intercepts every request before FastMCP's own OAuth middleware layer.' Add an observable oracle to the integration AC: assert in the test that the middleware wraps at the outermost ASGI layer (e.g., the middleware's scope-mutating logic is observable when using httpx `ASGITransport` pointed at the wrapped app).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact header set constituting the 'X-Forwarded-* family' that the middleware strips (e.g., X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Host, X-Forwarded-Proto, X-Forwarded-Port, X-Forwarded-Prefix, X-Forwarded-Server, X-Forwarded-Ssl), or specify a matching rule (e.g., 'any header whose lowercased name starts with `x-forwarded-`'), so the security boundary and AC3's test oracle are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan references the 'entire X-Forwarded-* family' for stripping but never enumerates which headers are members, leaving the security boundary and the test oracle undefined.
findings.location: What section: 'It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family (and the configured identity/secret headers)'; Security section: 'rebar additionally strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family on its side unless the shared secret validates'; AC line: 'that the entire X-Forwarded-* family is stripped in that case'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'entire X-Forwarded-* family' with: 'any header whose lowercase name begins with `x-forwarded-` (prefix-match, after normalizing `-`/`_` and case), plus the configured `auth_proxy_secret_header` and `auth_proxy_identity_header`'. This makes the stripping rule both implementable and testable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete location(s) where the operator prerequisite warning will live: e.g., a dedicated 'Trusted-proxy deployment checklist' subsection in the README/operator guide AND/OR a startup log WARNING emitted when the proxy strategy is active.
findings.finding: The 'operator prerequisite' (the fronting proxy MUST overwrite/strip all client-supplied identity and forwarded headers) is described as 'documented, loud' but the plan does not specify WHERE this warning will appear — no target doc, README section, or startup log message is named.
findings.location: ## Security — 'Operator prerequisite (documented, loud)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or 'What' bullet naming the target: 'a WARNING-level startup log line when `proxy` is in `auth_strategies` reminding operators that the fronting proxy must strip client-supplied forwarded headers; and a deployment-prerequisites subsection in docs/operators.md (or README Security section).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete wiring point where the trusted-proxy ASGI middleware wraps the FastMCP app in the HTTP deployment path (e.g., reference whether FastMCP exposes a `.asgi_app` property, a Starlette app, or another ASGI callable), and confirm this interface is stable in the installed mcp library version.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that an ASGI middleware can be layered in front of the FastMCP MCP server for HTTP deployments, but the current MCP server runs over stdio (`build_server().run()`) and the plan does not specify how the middleware wraps FastMCP's ASGI interface in the HTTP deployment context. It is unverified whether FastMCP exposes a standard ASGI `__call__` interface that a custom middleware can wrap, and how that wiring is exposed in the rebar entrypoint.
findings.location: What section: 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'; Integration test AC: 'httpx ASGITransport'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the transport-protection contract for the proxy→rebar internal leg: either (a) state that the proxy verifier REQUIRES loopback-only bind (and fail-closed at startup if `auth_proxy_*` is configured with a non-loopback bind), or (b) explicitly state that the non-loopback path requires TLS on the internal leg (e.g., mutual TLS or an encrypted overlay) and add that requirement to the S1 non-loopback gate conditions.
findings.finding: The transport-protection posture of the proxy→rebar internal HTTP leg is undeclared. The shared secret travels as a plain HTTP header on this hop; for non-loopback deployments (explicitly permitted by S1's gate) this is a cleartext secret over a network segment. The plan defers to 'S1's existing non-loopback gate' without stating that gate specifically requires TLS on the proxy→backend leg.
findings.location: Security section — 'Loopback bind is the intended deployment (S1 default); a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'When `proxy` is in `auth_strategies` and the server bind is non-loopback, startup MUST either (a) reject the combination as insecure unless an explicit `auth_proxy_allow_non_loopback` acknowledgment key is set, or (b) require that the S1 non-loopback gate's TLS-at-edge ack covers the proxy→rebar leg specifically. Document that the x-proxy-auth secret header MUST NOT traverse an unencrypted network segment.' This closes the undeclared posture on the internal leg where the secret actually lives.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each S2-delivered construct (`TokenVerifier`, `AccessToken`, `auth_resource_server_url`, `auth_strategies`, the composite verifier loop), either verify S2 is committed and contains these exact interfaces (Grep/Read the S2 branch or link the S2 AC that defines the contract), or make S5 explicitly define the interfaces it needs from S2 as a formal prerequisite contract.
findings.finding: The plan's entire architecture rests on `TokenVerifier`, `AccessToken`, `auth_resource_server_url`, `auth_strategies`, and a 'composite' auth framework all claimed to be 'introduced by S2' — but none of these constructs exist anywhere in the codebase, and the S2 ticket is not found in the tracker. These are codebase scope-exclusion claims ('S2 introduces X; S5 builds on it') that fail a Grep/Read probe.
findings.location: What section: 'activated by including `proxy` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**'; 'a TokenVerifier-shaped component'; 'returns an AccessToken'; 'resource=auth_resource_server_url'; 'flows through the **same composite**'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact S2-exported interface contract S5 depends on (module path, `TokenVerifier` method signature, `AccessToken` field names, composite runner None-passthrough behavior, and where `auth_resource_server_url` is introduced) so the S5 implementer has a verifiable integration surface and does not need to guess.
findings.finding: The plan's core seam — `mcp.auth_strategies`, the composite runner, the `TokenVerifier` protocol, `AccessToken` type, and `auth_resource_server_url` config key — is entirely absent from the current codebase (zero hits for `auth_strategies`, `TokenVerifier`, `AccessToken`, or `auth_resource_server_url`). All of these are asserted to come from S2, but S2 is not yet present. The plan cannot be implemented as described until S2 lands, and the plan does not specify the exact interface contract (method signatures, module location, field names) that S2 will export, leaving the integration surface unverified.
findings.location: What / Approach sections — 'the same composite contract as token verifiers', 'TokenVerifier-shaped component', 'resource=auth_resource_server_url', 'S2 seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Interface contract (from S2)' subsection listing: the import path for `TokenVerifier`/`AccessToken`, the exact method signature the verifier must implement, how the composite runner invokes it (e.g., `verify(request) -> AccessToken | None`), and whether `auth_resource_server_url` is a new McpConfig field introduced by S2 or S5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan which mechanism the middleware uses to pass the validated proxy identity to the verifier (e.g., 'store in ASGI `request.state.proxy_principal`; the verifier reads `request.state` and never reads raw headers'), so the executor makes no open-ended design decision on a security-critical channel.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the internal channel by which the ASGI middleware communicates the validated proxy identity to the verifier component (e.g., ASGI `request.state`, a context variable, or a sanitized synthetic header). For a security-critical path, this is a design decision the executor must make without guidance — choosing wrongly (e.g., a synthetic header that is also readable externally) could re-open the spoofing surface.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale — 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section: 'The middleware stores the validated identity in `request.state.proxy_principal` (an ASGI-standard per-request slot); the verifier reads only that slot and never re-reads raw request headers, ensuring no raw-header path survives into the verifier.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` (`build_server` and/or `main`) as an edit target in the plan's Scope/AC, and specify the ASGI wrapping seam (e.g. wrap `mcp.asgi_app()` or inject via FastMCP middleware hook).
findings.finding: The plan names `mcp_server.py`'s `build_server` / `main` as an implicit edit target (ASGI middleware must wrap the app 'before the MCP app') but does not enumerate it explicitly. The current `build_server()` returns a bare `FastMCP` instance and `main()` calls `build_server().run()` — adding proxy middleware requires modifying one or both of these functions, but neither is listed as an edit target in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the specific FastMCP ASGI surface the middleware wraps (e.g., `mcp.streamable_http_app()`, `mcp.get_asgi_app()`, or the underlying Starlette `app` attribute) and confirm it exists in the installed version, so the integration test's `ASGITransport` target is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan asserts an ASGI middleware wrapping FastMCP for the HTTP transport path, but the current `build_server()` returns a `FastMCP` instance that runs over stdio. The plan does not specify how the ASGI middleware is attached: whether it wraps `mcp.asgi_app()`, `mcp.streamable_http_app()`, or some other surface, and whether FastMCP's ASGI app is accessible in the installed version. This is a new integration surface (ASGI wrapping of FastMCP) that is unverified in the codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'a small ASGI middleware'; Integration test AC — 'Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the request-scoped principal storage mechanism explicitly (e.g., ASGI `scope` dict key or `contextvars.ContextVar`) so implementors cannot accidentally use a global/module-level variable that would cause cross-request identity contamination under concurrency.
findings.finding: The mechanism for storing the 'request-scoped principal' (extracted by the middleware, consumed by the verifier) is unspecified. In concurrent async ASGI code, using a module-level variable or any non-request-scoped store would cause cross-request identity contamination — a critical security flaw. The plan must specify the storage mechanism (ASGI scope dict or contextvars.ContextVar).
findings.location: What section: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Approach section: 'The middleware stores the verified principal in the ASGI `scope` dict under a namespaced key (e.g., `scope["rebar.proxy_principal"]`); the verifier reads from the same key. This is safe under concurrent async dispatch because each request has its own `scope` dict.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either reproduce the `TokenVerifier` protocol and `AccessToken` dataclass fields from S2 in this plan, or add an explicit cross-reference (e.g., 'implements `TokenVerifier` as defined in S2 — see `_mcp_auth.py:TokenVerifier`') so the implementer has a single authoritative source for the interface contract.
findings.finding: The plan references `TokenVerifier`-shaped component and `AccessToken` as the shared types/protocols the verifier must implement, but these are S2 artifacts that do not exist in the codebase and whose interface vocabulary (method signatures, field names, nullability) is never specified in this plan.
findings.location: What section: 'a TokenVerifier-shaped component so that proxy identity flows through the same composite'; 'the verifier turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken with client_id=the identity, scopes=auth_proxy_scopes, and resource=auth_resource_server_url'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the middleware's valid-secret behavior between 'What' and 'Security': explicitly specify in 'What' that the middleware strips all X-Forwarded-* and identity/secret headers from the request unconditionally, then re-injects only the verified identity into a request-scoped principal (not back onto headers) — and add an AC that tests this strip occurs even when the secret is valid.
findings.finding: The 'What' section only describes stripping identity/forwarded headers for the *invalid-secret* path, while the Security section uses 'strip-then-repopulate' language implying headers are also stripped (after extraction) in the *valid-secret* path. An implementer reading only 'What' would skip the post-extraction strip, leaving raw identity headers forwarded downstream.
findings.location: What section (middleware bullet) vs Security section (3rd hard requirement bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' middleware bullet, change 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal; otherwise it strips it. It strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family...' to 'unconditionally strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family plus the configured identity and secret headers; if the secret validated, it re-injects the verified identity as a request-scoped principal (not as a header).' Add an AC: 'Unit test asserts that even for a valid-secret request, the raw X-Forwarded-User and X-Forwarded-* headers are removed from the forwarded request (strip-then-repopulate; headers are never passed through as-is).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC2, replace 'valid auth_proxy_secret header' with unambiguous terminology, e.g. 'with the secret header (named by auth_proxy_secret_header, default x-proxy-auth) set to the value of the env var named by auth_proxy_secret_env'.
findings.finding: AC2 uses the phrase 'valid auth_proxy_secret header' which conflates the config key name ('auth_proxy_secret_env', which stores the ENV VAR NAME, not the secret itself) with the header name (configured by 'auth_proxy_secret_header'). A reader/implementer cannot unambiguously determine which entity 'auth_proxy_secret header' refers to.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: change 'with a valid auth_proxy_secret header' to 'with the configured secret header (auth_proxy_secret_header, default x-proxy-auth) containing the correct secret (from the env var named by auth_proxy_secret_env)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing: (a) the decision to route proxy identity through the composite rather than bypass it; (b) the header-strip-then-repopulate strategy and why it defeats spoofing; (c) the operator prerequisite (proxy MUST overwrite client-supplied forwarded headers) and where that operator-facing warning lives (e.g. README security section).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, security-critical architectural decision (trusted-proxy pattern: header-stripping middleware + proxy identity routed through the composite audience check rather than around it) but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the decision and its rationale. S7 only covers env-var/config doc regeneration, not architectural decision records.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale, ## Security, ## Acceptance Criteria (AC1 — 'Docs regenerated by S7')
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-trusted-proxy-verifier.md` (or equivalent) covering: decision, context (CVE references already in the plan), the composite-routing rationale, and the operator deployment prerequisite. Reference it from the operator/deployment docs so it is discoverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: specify that failed secret comparisons and header-stripping events are logged at WARN/ERROR level (with no secret value in the log), so operators can detect spoofing attempts and misconfiguration; add a corresponding AC asserting this log output.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no runtime observability (logging/metrics) for security-relevant events: failed shared-secret comparisons, header-stripping events, and proxy-auth denials. Operators cannot detect spoofing attempts, misconfigured proxies, or brute-force attacks on the secret without this instrumentation.
findings.location: Security section; AC — no observability AC present
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Unit/integration test asserts that a request with an invalid or missing proxy secret produces a WARN-level log entry (with no secret value) and that a stripped X-Forwarded-* header produces a DEBUG/WARN entry; no log entry is produced for valid authenticated requests (to avoid noise).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (unit or integration) asserting that a request with a valid shared-secret header but an absent or empty identity header is denied (401/None from verifier) and emits a detectable diagnostic, to cover the proxy-misconfiguration failure path.
findings.finding: The testing plan has no explicit AC covering the edge case where the shared-secret header IS valid but the identity header is absent or empty — a realistic proxy misconfiguration scenario that produces a distinct failure path (verifier returns None → composite denies).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (unit test) and AC6 (integration test)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 or add a new AC: 'Unit test asserts: when the shared-secret header is valid but the configured identity header is absent (or empty-valued), the verifier returns None and the request is unauthenticated (401); observable oracle: 401 response, no AccessToken minted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and `docs/env-vars.md` as consumers requiring update (or extend `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` to cover the new auth env-read helper in `_mcp_auth.py`) so the env-registry drift gate stays green.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it statically scans `src/rebar` for env-var reads and generates `docs/env-vars.md`, gated by a CI drift check. The new `_mcp_auth.py` reads an env var whose *name* is itself supplied via config (`auth_proxy_secret_env`), which is an indirected read the scanner's static patterns cannot capture. The resulting `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_SECRET` (or whatever the operator names it) will not appear in `docs/env-vars.md`, but more critically, the env var value read *by* `auth_proxy_secret_env` may also be absent. The plan does not name this script or `docs/env-vars.md` as edit targets.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_secret_env` config key; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/config.md` to the plan's edit-target list and update the `# MCP server gates` section with the new `mcp.auth_proxy_*` keys (or explicitly note this is deferred to S7 with a reference).
findings.finding: `docs/config.md` manually documents `mcp.*` config keys (confirmed at line 135–138) but is not named as an edit target. Adding four new `mcp.auth_proxy_*` keys without updating this file leaves the operator-facing config reference stale.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_*` config fields; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unverifiable CVE citations ('CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484') with either verified, real published advisory links or restate the threat in terms of the well-documented forwarded-header spoofing class (e.g., reference the oauth2-proxy docs on trusted upstream headers, or OWASP on header injection) — so the threat model is grounded in citable, real prior art.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484' as threat-model justification for the header-stripping design. CVE-2026-40575 is a future-year identifier (2026) and CVE-2025-64484 does not appear in any codebase file or widely-known published advisory. Both look like unverifiable/synthetic identifiers. Citing fabricated or unverified CVEs in a security-critical plan misleads reviewers about the actual grounding of the threat model.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the CVE identifiers and replace with: 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class described in the oauth2-proxy upstream trust documentation and the general trusted-proxy pattern (see e.g. oauth2-proxy --trusted-ip / upstream-auth docs).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference the existing shared-secret guard implementations in `opcert_service/app.py` and `review_bot/app.py`, and either justify why a new ASGI middleware class is the right seam (e.g., it must run pre-routing before FastMCP tools are dispatched) or extract a shared `_guard_ok`-style utility that all three sites use.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new ASGI-middleware + shared-secret guard pattern without acknowledging the existing shared-secret guard implementations already in the codebase (`opcert_service/app.py` `_guard_ok` / `review_bot/app.py` token check). No justification is given for why a new middleware is the right seam rather than extending or sharing the existing guard utility.
findings.location: What section: middleware description; Approach & rationale

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Security requirement that the ASGI middleware MUST redact or omit the `auth_proxy_secret_header` value from any logging performed by the middleware itself, and add an AC or implementation note stating the header must not be echoed in error responses or debug traces.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that the raw `auth_proxy_secret` header value must be excluded from ASGI framework access logs and debug traces. If the ASGI framework (e.g., uvicorn, Starlette) logs all request headers at debug level, the shared secret will appear in logs, constituting a secret-in-logs exposure.
findings.location: Security section / What section — middleware description; no mention of log redaction
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section add: 'The middleware MUST NOT log the value of `auth_proxy_secret_header`; it may log presence/absence only. Error responses must not echo the header value. Implementation: use a log filter or explicitly avoid including request.headers in any structured log emit within the middleware.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC5, replace 'once the required scope is granted' with 'once auth_proxy_scopes is configured to include the required scope' to match the static-config model described in What.
findings.finding: AC5's observable oracle says 'vs 200 once the required scope is granted' — but the described feature uses a *static* scope set (auth_proxy_scopes is a flat config value), not a dynamic grant mechanism. The word 'granted' implies a runtime grant that doesn't exist.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 observable oracle: '403 with empty auth_proxy_scopes vs 200 once auth_proxy_scopes is set to include the required scope'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` is created by S2 (making it a hard prerequisite deliverable to verify) or by S5 (in which case the plan must specify the full new-file interface, not just the proxy additions).
findings.finding: The plan says 'Add a trusted-proxy mode to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py' implying the file is a pre-existing module to extend, but no such file exists in the repository — the directory listing and file search both confirm its absence. This mis-states the scope: S5 must CREATE the file, not extend it, which affects how scope and interface contracts are defined.
findings.location: What section: 'Add a trusted-proxy mode to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (and its `docs/mcp-reference.md` drift gate) as a consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS` that must be regenerated in S5 itself (or clarify how the CI drift gate is satisfied before S7 lands).
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS`: it pulls the env-var table directly from that tuple and its `--check` drift gate runs in CI (`test.yml:308`, `gerrit-verify.yaml:262`). Adding new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_*` entries to `MCP_ENV_VARS` will cause `docs/mcp-reference.md` to go stale, failing the CI drift gate. The plan defers doc regeneration to S7 without naming this script or its drift gate as a dependency.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet; 'Docs regenerated by S7'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to the S5 implementation steps, or note that `make test` will catch the drift and the author must regenerate before the PR merges.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `auth_proxy_scopes` is required, optional with a default (e.g., empty set), or causes a startup failure when absent/empty. This is security-relevant: silently granting empty scopes vs. failing closed are meaningfully different behaviors, and an executing agent cannot choose between them without guessing.
findings.location: What / config fields description and Security section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the config field description: 'auth_proxy_scopes: optional; defaults to empty set (no scopes granted); an empty value is valid and fail-safe — operators SHOULD set an explicit scope list. Startup does NOT fail on absence.' (or the opposite policy if fail-closed is intended, consistent with the secret-env-var behavior).

findings.finding: The `auth_proxy_scopes` config field has no specified optionality, default, or failure behavior. The plan is explicit that `auth_proxy_secret_env` must be present and non-empty at startup or the verifier refuses to start, but `auth_proxy_scopes` has no analogous rule. Since scopes gate what the proxy-authenticated principal can do, an absent or empty `auth_proxy_scopes` is security-relevant: does it grant no scopes (deny everything)? All scopes? Cause startup failure? This decision rule is absent.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_scopes` field description / Security section

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
